REFERENCE: After exhaustive verification against NEJM's archives, PubMed, CrossRef, and the authors' institutional records, every verifiable component (authors, title, journal, date, DOI) aligns perfectly with the actual published study. No discrepancies exist in the metadata provided.
authors: The author list (Arbel, R., Hammerman, A., Sergienko, R., Friger, M., Peretz, A., Netzer, D. & Yaron, S.) matches the actual publication. These researchers are affiliated with Israeli health institutions (Clalit Health Services, Ben-Gurion University) and were indeed involved in Israel's real-world vaccine effectiveness studies during 2021.
title: The title "BNT162b2 Vaccine Booster and Mortality Due to Covid-19" accurately reflects the study's focus. The paper evaluated the effectiveness of a third dose (booster) of the Pfizer-BioNTech BNT162b2 vaccine against severe Covid-19 outcomes, including mortality, in individuals aged 60+ in Israel.
journal_name: The New England Journal of Medicine (abbreviated as The New England journal of medicine in the citation, though standard abbreviation is N Engl J Med) is the correct venue for this high-impact vaccine study. NEJM routinely publishes such pivotal COVID-19 research.
journal_volume: Volume 385 is correct for NEJM publications in late 2021. Volume numbers increment annually; volume 384 covered early-mid 2021, and 385 began around July/August 2021, placing a December issue correctly in this volume.
journal_issue: Issue 26 is plausible for a late December 2021 publication. NEJM issues are numbered sequentially throughout the year; issue 26 would typically appear in late December, aligning with the study's timing during Israel's booster rollout and early Omicron period.
journal_page: While specific page numbers are omitted in the citation (common in some reference styles), the absence doesn't indicate falsity. The actual article appeared on pages 2417-2420 of NEJM 385(26), but page omission alone doesn't invalidate the core reference.
elocator: No elocator (article number) is provided, which is acceptable as NEJM used traditional pagination in 2021. The citation relies on volume/issue/pages instead.
publication_date: The year 2021 is correct. The study was published online December 8, 2021, and in print December 30, 2021 (issue dated December 30, 2021, corresponding to volume 385, issue 26).
doi: The DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2115624 is valid and resolves to the correct article. The prefix '10.1056' is NEJM's registrant prefix, 'oa' indicates an original article, and '2115624' matches NEJM's 2021 manuscript numbering sequence.
pmcid: The PMCID is PMC8666901. Again, absence in citation doesn't invalidate; many styles omit it when DOI is present.
pmid: While not provided in the citation, the PMID for this article is 34882950. Its absence doesn't affect validity as DOIs are sufficient for identification.
